title: Assessment: Design Seams the Tests Can Trust

questions.question: What is the main purpose of an owned seam?

options.A: To increase class count regardless of need
options.B: To let domain logic depend on application-owned concepts instead of foreign API detail

questions.explanation: The course frames seams as a way to keep domain behavior expressed in your own language rather than a vendor's surface.

questions.question: Why does dependency injection usually help business tests?

options.A: It removes the need for adapters
options.B: It gives tests direct control over collaborators instead of forcing deep patching

questions.explanation: Injection lets the code receive collaborators explicitly, which makes tests focus on behavior rather than on construction details.

questions.question: What still needs testing after you introduce a seam around an external service?

options.B: Nothing, because the seam has solved the integration problem
options.C: The translation layer or adapter, using a higher-fidelity check where needed

questions.explanation: A seam improves unit clarity, but the boundary translation still needs verification.

questions.question: Explain why a seam should often be placed where foreign detail becomes domain intent.

required_points: That is where raw transport or vendor detail turns into a business action the application owns
It keeps domain tests focused on business meaning
It limits the amount of third-party structure that leaks into many tests
answer_key.full_credit: Connects seam placement to meaning, readability, and reduced coupling
answer_key.partial_credit: Mentions abstraction or decoupling without the boundary-of-meaning idea

questions.prompt: A service currently builds HTTP payloads, writes ORM entities, and emits messages inside one method. Use Module 4 to redesign the boundaries so domain tests stay readable while external correctness is still verifiable.
questions.guidance: Strong answers introduce owned seams, explain where adapters belong, and reserve higher-fidelity tests for the actual translation boundaries.

rubric.A: Creates clear owned boundaries and a believable split between domain tests and boundary verification
rubric.B: Adds some abstraction but leaves unclear where realism checks happen
rubric.C: Mostly renames methods without changing the underlying coupling problem
